question: What are the essential bit manipulation tricks for interviews?
quickAnswer: Key tricks: check bit i: (n >> i) & 1. Set bit i: n | (1 << i). Clear bit i: n & ~(1 << i). Toggle bit i: n ^ (1 << i). Check power of 2: n > 0 && (n & (n-1)) == 0. Count set bits: n & (n-1) removes lowest set bit. XOR of a with itself = 0; XOR with 0 = a. Used in: find single number, missing number, sum without addition.
keyPoints:
n & (n-1): clears lowest set bit (count set bits, check power of 2)
XOR properties: a^a=0, a^0=a, XOR is commutative/associative
n & (-n): isolates lowest set bit
>> arithmetic shift (sign-preserving); >>> logical shift (zero-fill)
Single number: XOR all elements — pairs cancel, single survives
Missing number: XOR all indices and values — missing cancels out
followUpQuestions: Why does XOR of all elements find the single non-duplicate?
How do you swap two numbers without a temporary variable using XOR?
How do you find two non-duplicate numbers when all others appear twice?
spokenAnswer: Bit manipulation tricks eliminate temporary variables and reduce constant factors. The most powerful is XOR: a number XOR'd with itself is 0, and XOR with 0 gives back the number. To find the single element in a pairs-only array, XOR everything — all pairs cancel to 0, leaving the single. n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit — loop until 0 to count set bits. The power-of-2 check (n & (n-1)) == 0 works because powers of 2 have exactly one bit set.
commonMistakes: Using >> instead of >>> for logical shifts — >> preserves the sign bit, giving negative values for negative inputs. Not knowing that n & (n-1) clears only the LOWEST set bit, not all set bits. Forgetting the n > 0 check for power of 2 — 0 would incorrectly pass the (n & (n-1)) == 0 check.
seniorExpectation: A senior candidate knows the two-non-duplicate extension: XOR all to get x = a^b, find any set bit to partition elements into two groups, XOR each group to isolate a and b. They'd apply bit manipulation for bitmask DP, subset enumeration (iterate from 0 to 2^n - 1), and flag manipulation.

question: How do you count the number of set bits (1s) in an integer?
quickAnswer: Brian Kernighan's algorithm: while n != 0, do n = n & (n-1) and increment count. n & (n-1) removes the lowest set bit each time. This is O(number of set bits) — faster than checking each of 32 bits when count is small. Java's Integer.bitCount() is the standard library method. Full loop with n >>> 1 is O(32) = O(1).
keyPoints:
Brian Kernighan: n & (n-1) removes lowest set bit, O(k) where k = set bit count
Naive: loop 32 times, check each bit with n & 1, n >>>= 1
Integer.bitCount(n): JDK method using Hamming weight algorithm
Lookup table: O(1) with precomputed table for nibbles or bytes
Applications: Hamming distance, count bits for 0 to n (DP using dp[i] = dp[i>>1] + (i&1))
followUpQuestions: How would you count set bits for all numbers from 0 to n using DP?
What is Hamming distance and how do you compute it?
Why is Brian Kernighan's algorithm faster than the naive loop for sparse bit patterns?
spokenAnswer: Brian Kernighan's insight: n & (n-1) clears the rightmost 1 bit. Loop until n is 0, incrementing count each time. For a number with k set bits, only k iterations run — much faster than always checking all 32 bits. For counting set bits from 0 to n: use DP with dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1) — right shift removes the last bit (count already known), plus 1 if the last bit was set.
commonMistakes: Using n >>= 1 instead of n >>>= 1 in the naive loop — arithmetic shift extends the sign bit for negative numbers, creating an infinite loop. Not knowing Integer.bitCount() exists in Java — reinventing the wheel in a timed interview. Forgetting that Brian Kernighan is O(k) not O(log n) or O(32).
seniorExpectation: A senior candidate applies the DP approach for counting bits from 0 to n (O(n) with O(n) space), knows the lookup table technique for O(1) per query with O(256) preprocessing, and uses Integer.bitCount() in Java knowing it compiles to a POPCNT instruction on modern hardware.

question: What makes a problem solvable by a greedy algorithm?
quickAnswer: A problem is solvable by greedy if it has: (1) Greedy choice property — a locally optimal choice leads to a globally optimal solution. (2) Optimal substructure — optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems. Proving greedy requires showing the exchange argument: swapping a non-greedy choice with the greedy one doesn't improve the solution.
keyPoints:
Greedy choice property: local optimum leads to global optimum
Optimal substructure: optimal solution built from optimal sub-solutions
Exchange argument: proof technique — show swapping in greedy choice doesn't improve
When greedy fails: 0/1 Knapsack (use DP); coin change with arbitrary coins
Greedy examples: Activity Selection, Huffman Coding, Prim's/Kruskal's MST, Dijkstra
Fractional knapsack: greedy works (take highest value/weight ratio first)
followUpQuestions: How does the exchange argument prove greedy correctness?
Why does greedy work for fractional knapsack but not 0/1 knapsack?
How do you decide between greedy and DP for an optimization problem?
spokenAnswer: Greedy works when making the locally best choice at each step leads to the globally best outcome. The exchange argument is the proof technique: assume the optimal solution makes a different choice, show that swapping in the greedy choice doesn't make it worse — therefore the greedy choice is also optimal. When greedy fails, you need DP — the local best choice doesn't consider future consequences. Fractional knapsack (take highest value/weight ratio) is greedy; 0/1 knapsack (can't split items) is DP.
commonMistakes: Applying greedy to problems where it looks like it works but doesn't — coin change with arbitrary denominations. Not formally proving the greedy choice property — assuming it works without verification. Not knowing that Dijkstra IS a greedy algorithm (always take the closest unvisited node).
seniorExpectation: A senior candidate applies the exchange argument formally for activity selection and can explain why it fails for 0/1 knapsack. They'd connect greedy to matroids (a mathematical structure where greedy always works) and discuss greedy approximation algorithms for NP-hard problems.

question: How do you solve the interval scheduling / activity selection problem?
quickAnswer: Sort intervals by end time. Greedily select the interval with earliest end that doesn't overlap the previously selected one. This gives the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals. Time O(n log n) for sort, O(n) for selection. The greedy choice of earliest end time is optimal — proven by exchange argument.
keyPoints:
Sort by end time: key insight
Greedy: always pick earliest-finishing non-overlapping activity
Correctness: choosing earliest end leaves maximum room for future activities
Variant: merge overlapping intervals — sort by start, merge if start <= prevEnd
Min platforms/rooms: sort starts and ends separately, two-pointer
Interval DP: different beast — for problems like matrix chain multiplication
followUpQuestions: Why do we sort by end time and not start time?
How do you find the minimum number of rooms/platforms needed (no conflicts)?
How do you merge overlapping intervals?
spokenAnswer: The key insight is sorting by end time. The greedy argument: if we have two non-overlapping intervals A and B where A ends earlier, choosing A over B leaves more room for future intervals (B's later end time blocks more). This is the exchange argument — swapping any non-greedy choice with the greedy one doesn't decrease the count. For minimum rooms needed: it's the maximum overlap at any point — sort starts and ends separately, use a two-pointer to count concurrent intervals.
commonMistakes: Sorting by start time for maximum non-overlapping intervals — doesn't give the optimal greedy choice. Confusing minimum intervals to remove with maximum non-overlapping (they're complements). Not knowing the minimum rooms variant — it's a different algorithm.
seniorExpectation: A senior candidate solves all interval variants: maximum non-overlapping (greedy by end), minimum rooms (sweep line or sort starts/ends), meeting scheduler (find gaps), and interval merging. They'd connect this to the scheduling theory and know when interval DP is needed instead of greedy.

question: What is Huffman coding and why is it a greedy algorithm?
quickAnswer: Huffman coding builds an optimal prefix-free code for data compression. Greedy strategy: always merge the two smallest-frequency nodes. Use a min-heap, extract two minimums, merge into a parent with combined frequency, push back. Characters with higher frequency get shorter codes. Greedy works because merging least frequent nodes minimizes the total cost (weighted path length).
keyPoints:
Min-heap: always pick two smallest frequency nodes
Merge: create parent with sum frequency, push back
Prefix-free: no code is a prefix of another (Huffman guarantees this)
Optimality: greedy → minimum average code length
Applications: DEFLATE (ZIP), PNG, HTTP/2 header compression (HPACK)
Time: O(n log n) for building tree
followUpQuestions: How do you prove Huffman coding is optimal?
What is a prefix-free code and why is it important for decoding?
How is Huffman coding used in DEFLATE (ZIP) compression?
spokenAnswer: Huffman coding assigns shorter bit sequences to more frequent characters. The greedy insight: always merge the two lowest-frequency nodes. The resulting tree has frequent characters closer to the root (shorter paths/codes) and rare characters deeper (longer codes). The exchange argument proves optimality: merging any other pair instead of the two smallest would result in a higher total weighted path length. This is the algorithm behind ZIP, PNG, and HTTP/2 header compression.
commonMistakes: Confusing Huffman coding (variable-length, greedy) with fixed-length coding (no compression). Not knowing that the Huffman tree must be rebuilt and sent alongside compressed data — or that canonical Huffman avoids this. Not connecting this to real compression formats used in production.
seniorExpectation: A senior candidate implements the full algorithm using a PriorityQueue, proves optimality via the exchange argument, and discusses canonical Huffman (deterministic tree reconstruction from code lengths without sending the full tree). They'd connect to Arithmetic coding as the information-theoretically optimal successor.
